chunks.page_summary: This page provides a troubleshooting guide for Modbus TCP/IP communication issues with Rheonics inline viscometers and density sensors, outlining steps to verify network settings using ping tests.
chunks.extracted_text:
Phone: +1 713 234 0863
Modbus TCP/IP Troubleshooting Guide
Modified on: Thu, 16 Jan, 2025 at 11:48 PM

1. Overview
This article provides a comprehensive approach to troubleshooting Modbus TCP/IP communication issues, specifically for engineers and technicians working with Rheonics Inline Viscometers (https://rheonics.com/products/inline-viscometer-srv/) and Density-Viscosity Meters (https://rheonics.com/products/inline-density-meter-srd/). Rheonics sensors support out-of-the-box Modbus TCP/IP communication, ensuring seamless integration into customer processes.

2. Network Settings
* Make sure you can ping the Rheonics SME (https://rheonics.com/electronics-and-communication/). On Windows, open the command prompt and use the ping command to test the connectivity between the PC and Rheonics Viscometer (as shown on Figure 1).

C:\Users\______>ping 192.168.3.6
Pinging 192.168.3.6 with 32 bytes of data:
Reply from 192.168.3.6: bytes=32 time=1ms TTL=64
Reply from 192.168.3.6: bytes=32 time=1ms TTL=64
Reply from 192.168.3.6: bytes=32 time=1ms TTL=64
Reply from 192.168.3.6: bytes=32 time=1ms TTL=64
Ping statistics for 192.168.3.6:
Packets: Sent = 4, Received = 4, Lost = 0 (0% loss),
Approximate round trip times in milli-seconds:
Minimum = 0ms, Maximum = 1ms, Average = 0ms
C:\Users\______>

Figure 1. Ping Command Success

There are two possible outcomes for a ping test:

2.1 There is a response from IP Address
If there is a reply from the IP address, the network settings for both the PC and the Rheonics Sensor are configured correctly. Any issues with Modbus communication would not be caused by Ethernet or TCP/IP.

2.2 There is no response from the IP Address
If there is no response from the IP address, follow these steps to troubleshoot:
* Verify Network Settings on the PC (https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/articles/81000397449) : Ensure that the PC's network settings, such as IP address and subnet mask, are correctly configured.
* Check the Rheonics Sensor's Network Settings (https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/articles/81000297177) : Use the Rheonics Control Panel (RCP) to confirm the sensor's IP address and network configuration.
* Test with a Different Ethernet Cable: Replace the existing Ethernet cable with a known working cable or use a network cable tester (Figure 2) to rule out wiring issues.
key_facts: This guide provides a comprehensive approach to troubleshooting Modbus TCP/IP communication issues for Rheonics Inline Viscometers and Density-Viscosity Meters.
Rheonics sensors support out-of-the-box Modbus TCP/IP communication.

A successful ping test between a PC and a Rheonics Viscometer indicates that network settings for both devices are configured correctly and that Modbus communication issues are not due to Ethernet or TCP/IP.

If a ping test yields no response, troubleshooting steps include verifying the PC's network settings (IP address, subnet mask), checking the Rheonics Sensor's network settings using the Rheonics Control Panel (RCP), and testing with an alternative Ethernet cable.

chunks.page_summary: This page provides detailed instructions and troubleshooting for configuring Modbus settings, handling CRC errors, and utilizing Modbus debugging tools with Rheonics sensors.
chunks.extracted_text:
Figure 2. Ethernet Cable Tester

3. Modbus Settings

3.1 Verify the Modbus TCP Port

*   Ensure that the Modbus server is using the default port (502). Refer to the following article for details on factory settings: Connecting the Modbus TCP outputs (https://support.rheonics.com/support/solutions/articles/81000297604)
*   Check that port 502 is open on firewalls between the PC/Modbus TCP Client and the Rheonics Sensor.

3.2 Check Slave Address Configuration

*   Confirm that the Rheonics Sensor’s Modbus slave address is set to 255 (Default value).

3.3 Validate Input Register Address

*   Check and validate Rheonics Input Register addresses. Make sure the data types and the address are correct.
*   Depending on the PLC or tool being used to read the Input Register, ensure the correct indexing method is being used. Some PLCs or tools are zero-indexed, requiring you to subtract 1 from the register address (e.g., 300137 becomes 136), while others are one-indexed, where the address is used as-is (e.g., 300137).

Register 300137 corresponds to Parameter 12: Viscosity Last Good (Float), as shown in Rheonics Input Register (https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/articles/81000297604).

4. CRC Errors

CRC (Cyclic Redundancy Check) errors indicate that the device is detecting bit activity on the network line, but the received bits do not form data that makes sense to the device. When encountering CRC errors, check the following:

*   Ensure that the Pre-Delay is set to at least 50 ms.

5. Modbus Debugging Tools

Modbus Poll (https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/articles/81000388169) is a Modbus master simulator that helps test and verify Modbus communication. It allows real-time monitoring of parameters like viscosity, density, and temperature (Figure 3).

Modbus Poll is particularly useful for:

*   Checking if registers are accessible and correctly configured.
*   Validating data types, such as integers or floats, against expected values.

Modbus Poll can be downloaded from this Dropbox link (https://www.dropbox.com/sh/lv76vwm5lnpy79b/AACL7dhglydyX_QcV7GK5lr1a?dl=0) and instructions on how to use Modbus Poll can be found at Rheonics Support Article: Quick Modbus TCP verification using Modbus Poll master software (https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/articles/81000388169).

Figure 3 shows Rheonics Input Registers on Modbus Poll, after using Rheonics ModbusPoll pre-configured files (https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/articles/81000397127).
key_facts: The Modbus server must use the default port 502, and this port needs to be open on firewalls between the PC/Modbus TCP Client and the Rheonics Sensor.
The Rheonics Sensor's Modbus slave address is configured to a default value of 255.

When reading Input Registers, it is crucial to ensure the correct indexing method is applied, as some PLCs are zero-indexed (requiring a subtraction of 1 from the register address) while others are one-indexed.
CRC errors signify that the device detects network activity but the received data is not valid, and can often be resolved by setting the Pre-Delay to at least 50 ms.
Modbus Poll is a Modbus master simulator software that aids in testing Modbus communication and monitoring parameters like viscosity, density, and temperature.
Modbus Poll is useful for verifying register accessibility, configuration, and validating data types.
warnings_notes: Ensure that the Modbus server is using the default port (502).
Check that port 502 is open on firewalls between the PC/Modbus TCP Client and the Rheonics Sensor.
Confirm that the Rheonics Sensor’s Modbus slave address is set to 255 (Default value).
Check and validate Rheonics Input Register addresses, ensuring data types and addresses are correct.
Depending on the PLC or tool, use the correct indexing method; some are zero-indexed (subtract 1 from address), others are one-indexed (use address as-is).
When encountering CRC errors, ensure that the Pre-Delay is set to at least 50 ms.

chunks.page_summary: This page explains how to correct Modbus TCP data interpretation issues, such as incorrect data types and endianness, by demonstrating the use of ModScan and ModbusPoll tools and providing a resolution for incorrect register indexing in ModScan.

6. Correcting Data Types and Endiannes

Typically, the error is a discrepancy between the Modbus TCP values and what is displayed on the sensor or the RCP. This is often due to an incorrect data type or improper endianness settings. To illustrate this, Figure 3 compares two tools used for testing Modbus TCP: ModScan (https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/articles/81000388169) and ModbusPoll (https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/articles/81000388169), highlighting a difference in Register 136 in red.

Figure 4. ModScan and ModbusPoll comparison

This is a common issue involving both incorrect indexing and data types. When using ModScan, selecting the correct starting address is crucial. For example, choosing Register 0040 as the starting address incorrectly applies the Float (MSRF) format to all registers, resulting in incorrect data being displayed.

This issue can be resolved by changing the starting address to 0041, which corresponds to the first Input Register. This ensures the Float format is correctly applied to the parameters.
key_facts: Modbus TCP errors can occur due to discrepancies between values displayed on the sensor or RCP and the actual Modbus TCP values.
These errors are often caused by an incorrect data type or improper endianness settings.

ModScan (https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/articles/81000416849) and ModbusPoll (https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/articles/81000388169) are two tools used for testing Modbus TCP.
A specific difference in data interpretation between ModScan and ModbusPoll is highlighted for Register 136, appearing in red in ModScan.
When using ModScan, incorrect indexing, such as choosing Register 0040 as the starting address, can incorrectly apply the Float (MSRF) format to all registers.
The incorrect application of Float format leads to the display of incorrect data.
The issue with incorrect data display in ModScan can be resolved by changing the starting address to 0041.
Changing the starting address to 0041 ensures the Float format is correctly applied because it corresponds to the first Input Register.

chunks.page_summary: This page displays a screenshot of the ModScan_RheonicsR software interface showing MODBUS input register values, which are identified as corrected viscosity values from a Rheonics device.
key_facts: The page displays MODBUS input register data from a Rheonics device using ModScan software.
The data shown represents corrected viscosity values.
The MODBUS connection is configured to read 125 input registers starting from address 0041 for Device ID 255.
The software interface includes menu options for File, Connection, Setup, View, Window, and Help.
